When the ruling party changed six years ago, the new administration systematically dismantled predecessor programs — not because they were ineffective, but as a political signal.
The government's unspoken rule when a new party takes power is: nothing from before works, everything must be destroyed. They spend a full year tearing down what was there.
The national entrepreneurship network, once a coordination hub, has become dysfunctional.

Where early events drew broad, cross-sector participation, communities have splintered into personality-branded groups. Jorge describes the shift from 'the Python community' to 'Pineda's Python community' — where individual ownership has replaced collective identity.
Establishing a bank in Guatemala is described as a "legally ultra-complex, expensive, and lengthy process that functions almost like a cartel."
Guatemala's president "lives on the edge of being removed because Congress won't let him work," making El Salvador-style executive-driven fintech legislation essentially impossible today.

Guatemala lost Intel to Costa Rica roughly 20 years ago when it lacked sufficient engineers, and Volkswagen chose Mexico over Guatemala more than a century ago for similar talent-pipeline reasons... reinforced by agricultural conglomerates whose political influence he describes as dominant.
Even committed employer-partners like GBM decline to hire graduates who lack prior professional experience.
We get this all the time when companies are selecting candidates: 'They don't have previous experience, and we need at least one year of experience.'

Corfo deploys capital as a senior loan with a first-lien repayment priority ahead of LPs — functioning effectively as a 1x liquidation preference — rather than as a true LP. This means a modestly successful fund returns nothing to equity LPs.
access to Corfo programs in practice requires hiring insiders who specialize in extracting Corfo funds, creating a rent-seeking layer that distorts program outcomes
Corfo/Startup Chile is structurally sound but operationally captured and poorly incentivized for results
Journalism schools trained reporters to serve local monopolies. That's still the dominant model today.

Cuantico's research found that VC associations in Latin America typically have only 20–30 members and are entirely consumed by basic operational costs from membership dues, leaving them no capacity to educate regulators, pension fund committees, or ministries.

the overwhelming majority of Latin American venture funds — over 90 percent of more than 350 funds in Cuantico's sample — are smaller than $15 million in size, well below the minimum ticket size of ~$50 million that pension funds require. GPs have not designed vehicles scaled or structured for institutional limited partners
One named Costa Rican fund, Parque Tec — acquired by Mexican operator Marcus Dantus — routinely takes 20% equity for tickets of $20,000–$40,000, implying a $100,000–$200,000 valuation.

A second fund requires a residential property as collateral on checks of $120,000. Both practices destroy cap tables, signal founder naivety to outside investors, and make follow-on institutional investment nearly impossible.
A medical-device AI startup called iNova lost its entire investor pipeline at Mexican pitch events solely because of a contaminated cap table from an early Parque Tec deal.
Costa Rica's state development banking system has deployed over $50 million in the past decade into non-scalable microenterprises — jam sellers, flower vendors — rather than technology startups.
findings.target_name: Costa Rica's state development banking system

findings.quote: There's a fund in Costa Rica that, in exchange for tickets of twenty to forty thousand dollars, asks for twenty percent equity. And there are Costa Rican startups that went along with it.
It's not venture capital. They're playing a VC game without VC rules. And when a real VC sees it, they don't like it — because it doesn't have VC conditions.

findings.quote: Universities in Latin America see themselves as there to extract capital from companies, not inject it.
Governments measure how many new businesses are created, but they make it impossible to close a business.

The regulatory environment for business closure in Mexico is described as a fundamental drag on entrepreneurship: there is no streamlined fiscal or corporate pathway to wind down a company, partners cannot easily absorb and declare losses, and the process is slow and uncertain enough that founders simply leave companies as legal shells rather than formally closing them.

Government partnerships tend to misalign on objectives — officials measure new business registrations rather than outcomes, and often want unicorns without investing in the thousands of smaller companies that must exist beneath them in the return-distribution pyramid.
CAF's current processes can take two to three years to deploy even a $10M fund commitment — a pace that is incompatible with early-stage venture dynamics.

Development banks are too slow and too heavy to be effective early-stage LPs. Current processes take two to three years to deploy commitments as small as $10M, and heavy reporting requirements create disproportionate friction for small fund managers.
SoftBank's excessive capital deployment between 2017 and 2019 over-funded many strong Latin American companies at inflated valuations.

They mandate 40-page requirements documents with no empirical track record, demand minimum checks of $10 million at 20% fund ownership (implying a $50–100 million minimum fund size), and prioritize documentation over experimentation.

Bimbo Ventures, which Menendez describes as treating CVC as a pre-acquisition screening mechanism indifferent to financial returns.

Multilaterals that keep funding these vehicles are actively distorting the market.
Top-quartile venture capital typically does not outperform the S&P 500. Roughly 60% of funds fail to return initial capital; about 30% return 1–3× over 10–12 years.
LPs modeling IRRs on a 10-year clock are systematically underestimating how long capital will be locked up.
Many GPs drift toward whatever is generating markups rather than maintaining their stated thesis.
Most LPs want to be the last check into a fund.
DFI-backed partial guarantees that allow banks to evaluate portfolio health rather than fixed collateral could unlock capital that is already allocated but sitting idle.

local bank credit committees apply traditional collateral requirements — personal guarantees, land titles — that disqualify asset-rich, cash-flow-positive companies like Albedo. Albedo's solar lease portfolio, a strong liquid asset, carries almost no weight in local bank underwriting.
Albedo has been in an IDB facility process for approximately two years.
impact equity investors paradoxically demand higher risk and higher returns than Albedo's low-risk, moderate-return model offers
A lot of the impact funds are actually looking for higher risk and higher returns, but we provide lower risk and offer lower returns — still good, but not high teens. So we fall into a gap.

The Honduras Digital Challenge, which LiSalud credits as its launch platform in 2019, has shut down. Tech for Dev — a joint initiative of UNITEC, the IDB, and Ficohsa — closed around 2023 under the previous administration.
An INCAE knowledge program also concluded.
Marjorie states flatly that as of early 2026, she is unaware of any active structured support program for tech startups in Honduras.
She describes the remaining entrepreneurial talent as dispersed and isolated, with some active in crypto communities in San Pedro Sula but with no centralizing institution.

When LiSalud explored bank financing, the best available rate was approximately 17% annually — assessed two to three years ago and believed to have risen further since. More fundamentally, Honduran banks lack a credit product category for digital-service businesses: loan officers classify them alongside photography studios or social-media agencies, requiring documentation frameworks designed for goods-based businesses.

Approximately eighteen months before the interview, LiSalud advanced to the third meeting stage with a Salvadoran accelerator (identified only by a triangular logo, offering approximately $100,000) before discovering the entire commitment was in-kind — legal advisory and technical consulting — rather than cash. The accelerator also requested approximately 8% equity in exchange. Marjorie rejected the deal because she needed cash to hire developers to build the features the accelerator required within a three-month acceleration window.
She describes this as a structural mismatch between what accelerators offer and what early-stage product companies actually need.
government programs in Honduras are generally perceived as politically captured and unreliable, which is why LiSalud has deliberately avoided participating in any government-run initiative.

arguing that current compliance costs actively discourage formalization and deter talented Hondurans — who can earn up to $20,000 per month working remotely for U.S. companies — from founding local ventures.
VAT filing is designed for product-based businesses, making compliance unnecessarily complex and costly for firms with no physical inventory.
Regulators tend to be way more conservative than what the law itself even requires.
even in more advanced markets like Mexico and Chile, regulators default to conservative interpretations that go beyond what the law actually requires, creating unpredictable friction.
Gallo dismisses the idea that creating GP/LP fund vehicles in Central American or Caribbean jurisdictions would attract meaningful VC capital, rating the impact close to zero.

Latitude attempted to standardize company formation and legal infrastructure across Brazil, Chile, Uruguay, Argentina, Mexico, and Colombia, and made meaningful progress before abandoning the effort due to insufficient transaction volume.

A lot of banks just don't do a very good job with the innovation economy here in the US. Transpose that to Latin America fifteen years ago, and there was nobody willing to talk to a startup — and if they did, they could add no value.

If you don't understand that startups by nature are going to be losing money for a while, and you have covenants that require a certain level of profitability — those startups just aren't going to be able to meet them. If the lender isn't patient and doesn't understand what they're getting into, it could be severe enough to cause the collapse of a company.
findings.target_name: Conventional lenders applying traditional covenants to venture-backed companies

findings.quote: Despite this innovation, Tsao says the product has not taken off as hoped, citing two structural obstacles: cultural discomfort with debt financing among Latin American founders and investors, and disproportionately high legal and documentation costs for loans typically under $5 million that span multiple national jurisdictions.

Costa Rica is a signatory to the Nagoya Protocol and has national legislation restricting patents on naturally occurring microorganisms and molecules... companies quietly take their research abroad to complete development, a workaround that is legally ambiguous and represents a significant ecosystem leakage problem.

If I discover an extremophile microorganism in the crater of Poás volcano with a wonderful effect on drought-stress resistance, I can't patent it — so it has no commercial value. What happens? Companies take the research to another country.
I have a case right now — this morning we had a meeting — where a group is offering a desperate startup convertible notes at eighteen percent in dollars. That's what they find quickly in the market.
